name: Vintage Biplane
description: A WW1-era biplane with stacked canvas wings, a tapered fuselage, a yellow engine cowling, a two-blade wooden propeller, and a swept tail fin.
feedback: The top-level-only constraint is the single biggest gotcha. I had to refactor my tail (fin + stabilizer) into one labelled `union()` because the moment I labelled the children instead of the parent, the labels vanished. A SCAD-source linter (or compile-time warning) that flagged `label()` calls nested inside `difference/intersection/union/hull` braces would save the trial-and-error.

`runScadAsync`'s result has a `labelMap`, but nothing tells you which labels were *expected and dropped*. Surfacing `lost: ['fin', 'stabilizer']` from labels the parser saw but provenance stripped would make it self-debugging — right now you have to diff the harness's MY_LABELS by hand.

First run takes ~10s for WASM warmup, ~2s per iteration after. A `--watch` mode that kept the engines hot and a printed thumbnail (PNG path, or even ANSI art) would shrink the 'edit-run-eyeball' loop. Right now you have to context-switch into the browser to verify proportions.

Per-label triangle counts are surfaced, but the propeller's thin blades got 1036 tris — most of them interior overlap with the cowling+fuselage hull. A `visibleSurfaceArea` figure (post-union, exterior only) would tell you whether a region is worth a distinct color or should be folded into a neighbor.

The label workflow docs cover the basics, but I had to guess that `for (sy = [-14, 14], sx = [-1.5, 4.5])` inside a top-level `label()` *does* preserve provenance (it does — it's a transformation, not a CSG op). A short table of 'survives provenance / strips provenance' for `for`, `if`, `mirror`, `intersection_for`, `minkowski` would shortcut a lot of that guessing.
